skill.name: bdi-mental-states
skill.description: Guides BDI mental state modeling by mapping RDF context into beliefs, desires, intentions, plans, justifications, and validation queries.
skill.summary: Model RDF-backed belief, desire, and intention structures for explainable cognitive agents.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high and medium hits, but review found they are largely false positives from Markdown code fences, Turtle/SPARQL syntax, ontology namespace URLs, and BDI terminology. No prompt injection, malicious instructions, bundled executable scripts, credential access, or unauthorized exfiltration behavior was found in the reviewed files. A low residual risk remains because reference examples include endpoint access and string-built SPARQL that require review before reuse.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick hits occur in Markdown fenced examples and inline ontology terms, not in executable skill code. No command execution instruction or bundled script was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is documentation syntax in Markdown code blocks. The reviewed skill contains no script file or instruction to run shell commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Ontology Namespace References
low_findings.description: The URL hits are RDF, OWL, XML Schema, DUL, BDI, FIPA, and example namespaces used in semantic web examples. They are identifiers, not evidence of data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear as namespace declarations inside RDF and SPARQL examples. No network call sends secrets or local data to those URLs.

low_findings.title: C2 and Weak Crypto Findings Are Semantic False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity blocker hits align with ordinary BDI and ontology vocabulary such as goals, beliefs, execution, and control flow examples. No encryption, hashing, command-and-control endpoint, or malware behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are part of documented agent architecture examples. The files do not contain crypto routines, obfuscation, persistence, or C2 communication logic.

low_findings.title: Reference Code Uses String-Built SPARQL Queries
low_findings.description: Framework examples construct SPARQL strings with interpolated URI and time values. This is documentation-only, but implementers should validate inputs or use safer query construction when adapting it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is clear in example code and could become an injection risk if copied with untrusted values. It is not active in the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Reference Examples Include User-Configured Network Endpoints
low_findings.description: The triple-store and FIPA examples show how to connect to an endpoint and share belief content with a receiver. This is expected for integration guidance but should be reviewed for access control in real deployments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples demonstrate network-capable integration patterns, but the endpoint and receiver are supplied by the user. No hardcoded collection endpoint or hidden exfiltration path was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: String Interpolation in SPARQL Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code builds SPARQL queries by interpolating URI and time values. This is not executed by the skill, but copied code should validate or safely encode inputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in a reference snippet and has a known injection class if reused carelessly. The risk is limited because it is not active code.

content.value_statement: Agent teams often need a clear way to explain why an action was chosen. This skill maps RDF context into BDI mental states with traceable beliefs, desires, intentions, plans, and justifications.
actual_capabilities: Defines BDI ontology concepts for beliefs, desires, intentions, goals, plans, world states, and mental processes.
Shows Turtle patterns for cognitive chains from perceived world states to committed intentions.
Provides SPARQL competency queries for validating mental state graphs and reasoning chains.
Explains T2B2T flow for translating triples to beliefs and projecting mental states back to triples.
Includes integration patterns for SEMAS rules, LAG pipelines, JADE, JADEX, triple stores, and FIPA ACL messages.
limitations: It provides modeling guidance and examples, not a packaged runtime or tested library.
Example code must be reviewed before production use, especially endpoint access and query construction.
It does not verify that generated RDF is logically complete for every domain.
It assumes users understand RDF, SPARQL, and agent architecture concepts.

use_cases.title: Design Explainable Agent Reasoning
use_cases.description: Map agent observations into beliefs, desires, intentions, and plans so decisions can be traced from evidence to action.

use_cases.title: Validate Semantic Agent Graphs
use_cases.description: Use competency questions to check whether mental state graphs contain required links, temporal bounds, and justifications.

use_cases.title: Prototype Multi-Agent Coordination
use_cases.description: Represent shared beliefs, coordinated intentions, and FIPA-style communication payloads for agent collaboration experiments.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic BDI Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Model this situation as BDI mental states: [describe the agent, observation, desired outcome, and intended action]. Include beliefs, desires, intentions, and justifications.

prompt_templates.title: Convert RDF Context to Beliefs
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these RDF facts into BDI beliefs and related world states. Preserve evidence links and add temporal validity where possible: [paste RDF summary].

prompt_templates.title: Design Competency Queries
prompt_templates.prompt: Create SPARQL competency questions for this BDI model. Cover belief support, desire motivation, intention fulfillment, plan ordering, and temporal validity.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Framework Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Map this BDI ontology design into an agent framework integration plan for [SEMAS, JADE, JADEX, or a triple store]. Include risks, validation points, and data flow.

output_examples.input: A commuter sees heavy traffic and wants to arrive at work by 9:00 AM.
output_examples.output: The skill would identify a traffic world state, a delay belief, an on-time arrival desire, an alternate-route intention, and a route plan with evidence.

output_examples.input: A team needs to check whether every intention in a graph fulfills a desire.
output_examples.output: The skill would propose a competency query and explain the expected empty-result validation pattern for missing fulfillment links.

output_examples.input: Two agents share a project deadline and need coordinated plans.
output_examples.output: The skill would model shared deadline beliefs, agent-specific desires, coordinated intentions, and communication content for belief sharing.

best_practices: Separate world states from mental states so beliefs clearly refer to external situations.
Attach justifications and validity intervals to mental entities that influence action.
Validate models with competency questions before using them for planning or coordination.
anti_patterns: Do not treat an intention as a direct action without an explicit plan and task sequence.
Do not omit supporting beliefs or justifications for desires and intentions.
Do not copy example endpoint or query code into production without validation and access controls.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create BDI mental state models using RDF, ontology patterns, and SPARQL validation queries.

faq.question: Does it execute agent plans?
faq.answer: No. It documents modeling patterns and integration examples. It does not include an executable agent runtime.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is written as guidance that these assistants can use when designing BDI models.

faq.question: What input works best?
faq.answer: Use structured context about agents, observations, goals, constraints, evidence, and planned actions.

faq.question: Does it require RDF knowledge?
faq.answer: Basic RDF and SPARQL knowledge helps because the examples use Turtle syntax and graph queries.

faq.question: Is the included code production ready?
faq.answer: No. The snippets are reference examples and should be reviewed, tested, and secured before implementation.
